Municipalities should avoid blanket defunding or automatic budget maintenance. Instead, audit outcomes, fund proven policing, and shift some money to mental health, housing, and violence prevention.

American municipalities should not simply keep police budgets the same or completely remove funding from police departments. A better approach is to reform how public safety money is spent. Cities should maintain enough funding for police to respond to serious crimes, but they should also move some money toward services that prevent crime and handle problems police are not always trained to solve.

Police officers play an important role in responding to violence, theft, emergencies, and dangerous situations. Communities need trained officers who can protect people and investigate crimes. If cities cut police budgets too much without a clear plan, response times could get worse, and residents may feel less safe.

At the same time, many problems that police are asked to handle are not really police problems. Issues like homelessness, drug addiction, mental health crises, and school conflicts often need social workers, counselors, medical workers, and housing support. Sending armed officers to every situation can sometimes make things worse. Funding these other services can reduce the number of emergencies in the first place.

Cities should also require better training, stronger accountability, and more transparency in police departments. Money should be spent on things that improve public trust, such as body cameras, de-escalation training, fair hiring practices, and independent review boards. If a department has a history of waste or misconduct, leaders should review its budget carefully instead of automatically increasing it.

The best solution is a balanced one. Police should be funded enough to handle serious crime, but cities should also invest more in prevention, mental health care, youth programs, and community services. Public safety is not just about arrests. It is also about building stable neighborhoods where fewer crimes happen in the first place.

The United States should move toward a single-payer healthcare system, but it should do so carefully and in stages.

A single-payer system would likely make healthcare simpler and more affordable for many people. Everyone would be covered, people would not lose insurance when they lose or change jobs, and the government could negotiate lower prices for medicine and services. It could also reduce the time and money spent dealing with many different insurance companies.

However, there are real concerns. Taxes would likely go up, even if many people no longer paid premiums or high out-of-pocket costs. Some workers in the private insurance industry could lose jobs. There could also be long wait times for some services if the system is not funded and managed well.

Keeping the private insurance market has some benefits, such as more plan choices and less disruption to the current system. But the current system leaves many people uninsured or underinsured, and costs are very high. Many families avoid care because they cannot afford it.

A good path would be to expand public coverage step by step, lower drug prices, protect people from surprise bills, and allow private insurance to exist only for extra coverage. The main goal should be simple: everyone should be able to get medical care without going broke.

The United States should abolish the Electoral College and elect presidents by a national popular vote. While the Electoral College was originally designed as a compromise among states and as a check on direct democracy, today it creates democratic problems that outweigh its benefits.

The strongest argument against the Electoral College is that it can allow the candidate who loses the popular vote to win the presidency. In a democracy, the person who receives the most votes nationwide should generally win the election. When the Electoral College produces a different result, it weakens public trust and makes some voters feel that the system is unfair.

The Electoral College also gives disproportionate influence to voters in swing states. Because nearly all states use a winner-take-all system, candidates focus most of their attention on a small number of competitive states such as Pennsylvania, Michigan, Wisconsin, Arizona, Georgia, and Nevada. Meanwhile, voters in strongly Democratic or Republican states are often ignored because the outcome there is predictable. A Republican in California or a Democrat in Alabama may feel that their presidential vote has little practical effect. A national popular vote would make every vote count equally, no matter where it is cast.

Supporters of the Electoral College argue that it protects smaller states and preserves the federal nature of the country. However, in practice, the current system does not primarily help small states; it helps competitive states. Many small states are safely Republican or Democratic and receive little campaign attention. A popular vote would not eliminate the importance of states in Congress or federalism generally. It would simply make the presidential election more directly democratic.

Another argument for keeping the Electoral College is that it encourages broad geographic support. But a national popular vote would still require candidates to appeal to many different groups and regions because votes everywhere would matter. Candidates could not afford to ignore rural voters, suburban voters, or urban voters if each additional vote contributed directly to the national total.

Abolishing the Electoral College would require a constitutional amendment, which would be difficult. An alternative is the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact, in which states agree to award their electoral votes to the winner of the national popular vote once enough states join. Either way, the goal should be the same: ensuring that the candidate with the most votes becomes president.

The Electoral College may have made sense as an eighteenth-century compromise, but modern presidential elections should reflect the principle of political equality. Every citizen’s vote should carry the same weight. For that reason, the United States should move to a national popular vote for president.

Colleges should generally **retain DEI programs**, but they should also **review and improve them** so they are fair, effective, and focused on helping all students succeed.

DEI programs can be useful because colleges bring together students from many backgrounds. Good DEI work can help reduce discrimination, support students who feel isolated, and make sure campus policies are fair. It can also prepare students to work in diverse workplaces after graduation.

However, colleges should not keep DEI programs without checking whether they actually work. Some people worry that DEI programs can become too political, create division, or use money without clear results. Colleges should take those concerns seriously.

A strong approach would be to keep DEI programs that:

- Help students feel safe and respected
- Support equal access to tutoring, advising, scholarships, and career help
- Teach respectful discussion across differences
- Follow the law and protect free speech
- Use clear goals and measure results

Colleges should eliminate or change DEI efforts that are poorly run, unfair, or mainly symbolic. The goal should not be to favor one group over another. The goal should be to make sure every student has a fair chance to learn and succeed.

So, colleges should **not simply eliminate DEI programs**, but they should **make them more transparent, balanced, and accountable**.

Neither blanket defunding nor unchanged budgets is ideal. Municipalities should fund effective policing while reallocating resources toward mental health response, violence prevention, housing, and accountability.

The United States should adopt universal coverage, potentially via single-payer or a tightly regulated public option, prioritizing affordability, access, administrative simplicity, and quality outcomes.

The United States should replace the Electoral College with a national popular vote for president.

The strongest reason is democratic legitimacy: the candidate who receives the most votes nationwide should win. A popular vote would treat every voter equally, regardless of whether they live in a swing state, a safe state, a large state, or a small state. It would also likely increase campaign attention beyond a handful of battleground states.

Arguments for keeping the Electoral College usually emphasize federalism, protection for small states, and stability. Those concerns are real, but in practice the current system does not primarily empower small states; it empowers competitive states. Voters in Wyoming, California, Alabama, and Massachusetts are largely ignored if the outcome there is predictable, while voters in Pennsylvania, Michigan, Wisconsin, Georgia, Arizona, and Nevada receive disproportionate influence.

A national popular vote would also reduce the chance of a president taking office despite losing the popular vote, which can damage public trust. It would not solve every problem in American elections, but it would make the presidential election more directly representative.

The best reform would be a constitutional amendment establishing a national popular vote, ideally paired with clearer national election standards and possibly ranked-choice voting or a runoff system to ensure the winner has broad support.
Municipalities should reform budgets, not simply defund or maintain them: reduce ineffective policing costs, invest in prevention and crisis services, and preserve accountable public-safety capacity.

Colleges should generally **retain DEI programs**, but they should also **review, reform, and measure them carefully** to ensure they are effective, legally compliant, and consistent with academic freedom.

DEI programs can serve important purposes: helping students from different backgrounds succeed, reducing discrimination, improving campus climate, and ensuring fair access to opportunities. Colleges have an interest in making sure students are not excluded or disadvantaged because of race, gender, disability, religion, socioeconomic status, veteran status, first-generation status, or other factors.

That said, DEI programs should not be immune from criticism. If a program is vague, ideologically coercive, poorly measured, or treats people unfairly based on identity, it should be changed or eliminated. DEI efforts are strongest when they focus on concrete goals such as:

- Equal access to advising, mentoring, and financial support  
- Disability accommodations and anti-harassment protections  
- Support for first-generation and low-income students  
- Fair hiring and admissions processes within legal limits  
- Campus climate initiatives that encourage respectful disagreement  
- Data-driven evaluation of student outcomes  

Colleges should avoid DEI practices that require political conformity, use rigid identity categories, or undermine merit-based standards. They should also be transparent about budgets, goals, and results.

So the best position is not “keep everything” or “abolish everything.” Colleges should **retain DEI programs that demonstrably promote fairness, inclusion, and student success**, while **eliminating or reforming programs that are ineffective, coercive, discriminatory, or legally questionable**.

The United States should not forcibly deport large numbers of undocumented immigrants. A better approach would be to allow most undocumented immigrants who have been living peacefully in the country to remain, while creating a structured path to legal status and focusing enforcement on genuine public-safety threats.

Mass deportation would be extremely disruptive. Millions of undocumented immigrants have lived in the United States for years, often working, paying taxes, raising children, and contributing to local communities. Many have U.S.-citizen children or spouses. Removing them in large numbers would separate families, destabilize communities, and create major labor shortages in industries such as agriculture, construction, hospitality, elder care, and food service.

It would also be very expensive and difficult to carry out. Deporting millions of people would require massive spending on enforcement, detention, courts, and transportation. It would likely lead to legal challenges and civil-liberty concerns, especially if enforcement sweeps affected people who are legally present or citizens.

At the same time, simply ignoring immigration law is not a good solution. A country has the right to control its borders and maintain a lawful immigration system. But enforcement should be targeted and practical. People who commit serious crimes, pose security threats, or recently crossed the border unlawfully may reasonably be prioritized for removal. Long-term residents with clean records should be treated differently.

A fair compromise would include several parts:

1. A path to legal status for undocumented immigrants who pass background checks, pay taxes or penalties, learn English if needed, and meet work or residency requirements.
2. Stronger and more efficient border management to reduce future illegal immigration.
3. Reform of legal immigration channels so employers and families have realistic, lawful options.
4. Tough penalties for employers who exploit undocumented labor.
5. Faster immigration courts so asylum and removal cases are resolved fairly and promptly.

The goal should be both lawful and humane. Mass deportation would punish families and communities on an enormous scale without solving the deeper problems in the immigration system. Allowing undocumented immigrants to remain under clear legal conditions would better serve the economy, public safety, and American values.

Colleges should retain DEI programs, but reform them to emphasize measurable outcomes, viewpoint diversity, fairness, transparency, and voluntary participation—ensuring inclusion without ideological coercion or lowered standards.

The United States should replace the Electoral College with a national popular vote.

A popular vote would be more democratic because every vote would count the same, no matter where a person lives. Under the Electoral College, candidates often focus mostly on swing states while ignoring states that are safely Democratic or Republican. This means voters in places like California, Texas, New York, or Alabama may feel their votes matter less in presidential elections.

A popular vote would also make the result easier to understand: the candidate with the most votes wins. This could help build trust in elections because the system would be simpler and more direct.

Supporters of the Electoral College argue that it protects smaller states and keeps candidates from focusing only on big cities. However, a national popular vote would still require candidates to win support from many kinds of voters across the country. Rural voters, suburban voters, and city voters would all matter equally.

The Electoral College was created for a different time. Today, the fairest system is one person, one vote. The president represents the whole country, so the winner should be the candidate who receives the most votes nationwide.

The better policy is generally **not to forcibly deport large numbers of undocumented immigrants**, but to **allow most long-term, nonviolent undocumented immigrants to remain** under a structured legal process.

A practical approach would be:

- **Legal status or a path to citizenship** for people who have lived in the U.S. for years, work, pay taxes, have family ties, and do not pose public-safety threats.
- **Prioritized enforcement** against people who commit serious crimes, traffic drugs or people, or present genuine security risks.
- **Stronger, more orderly immigration systems**, including faster asylum processing, more legal work pathways, and better border management.
- **Employer accountability**, so businesses cannot exploit undocumented labor while avoiding responsibility.
- **Integration requirements**, such as background checks, tax compliance, fees, and possibly English/civics standards for permanent status.

Mass deportation would be extremely costly, disruptive to families and communities, damaging to industries that rely on immigrant labor, and difficult to carry out without major civil-liberties concerns. At the same time, simply ignoring immigration law is not sustainable. A legalization-and-enforcement compromise is the most humane and practical option.

Academic freedom should generally prevail. Colleges exist to pursue truth, test ideas, and expose students to competing interpretations—not to reflect the preferences of whichever political party controls the government at a given moment. If officials can decide which classes are acceptable, higher education becomes vulnerable to censorship, ideological swings, and political pressure.

That does not mean colleges should have no accountability. Government has a legitimate role in setting broad rules, especially for public funding, civil rights protections, accreditation standards, financial transparency, and ensuring that degrees meet basic professional requirements in fields like medicine, law, engineering, and education. Public universities should also be accountable to taxpayers and students for quality, cost, and outcomes.

But there is a difference between setting broad standards and controlling curriculum. The government may require that a nursing program prepare students safely for clinical practice; it should not dictate the political or philosophical conclusions students must reach. It may fund programs in high-demand fields; it should not ban courses because their ideas are controversial.

Academic freedom protects both professors and students. It allows scholars to research unpopular subjects, challenge conventional wisdom, and teach complex issues honestly. It also helps students learn how to evaluate arguments rather than simply absorb approved viewpoints.

So the best approach is limited government oversight combined with strong academic independence. Colleges should be transparent and accountable, but decisions about what to teach should primarily be made by educators, scholars, and academic institutions—not politicians.

The United States should adopt a single-payer system for essential healthcare while allowing a limited private market for supplemental services. In practice, that means every resident would be covered by a public plan for medically necessary care—primary care, hospital care, emergency services, prescription drugs, mental health, maternity care, and preventive care—while private insurers could still sell coverage for extras such as private hospital rooms, elective conveniences, or faster access to nonessential services.

The strongest argument for single-payer is that the current U.S. system is expensive, fragmented, and unequal. The United States spends far more per person on healthcare than other wealthy countries, yet millions remain uninsured or underinsured. Many people avoid care because of deductibles, copays, or fear of surprise bills. A single-payer model would simplify coverage, reduce administrative waste, give the government stronger bargaining power over drug and hospital prices, and make access to care less dependent on employment or income.

Preserving the current private insurance market has advantages: it allows consumer choice, protects existing employer-based coverage for people who like it, and avoids a major disruption to insurers, hospitals, and workers in the healthcare administration sector. But those benefits come with serious costs. Private insurance creates complex billing systems, narrow provider networks, claim denials, and large administrative expenses. It also leaves many people vulnerable when they lose a job, change employers, get sick, or cannot afford out-of-pocket costs.

The main concern with single-payer is not whether universal coverage is desirable, but how to implement it responsibly. Taxes would rise, even if premiums and many out-of-pocket costs disappeared. Provider payment rates would need to be set carefully to avoid destabilizing hospitals, especially rural ones. The transition would also affect people employed by private insurers, so any reform should include job retraining, phased implementation, and administrative roles within the new system.

A reasonable path would be gradual rather than abrupt: expand Medicare or create a public plan open to all, automatically cover children and low-income adults, negotiate drug prices aggressively, cap out-of-pocket costs, and then move toward a universal public plan for essential care. This would reduce disruption while moving the country toward a simpler and fairer system.

So, the better choice is not to preserve the private insurance market as the foundation of American healthcare. The U.S. should make healthcare a universal public guarantee, with private insurance playing only a supplemental role.

The United States should abolish the Electoral College and use the national popular vote to choose the president.

The main reason is fairness. In a popular vote system, every person’s vote would count the same no matter where they live. Right now, voters in swing states often have more power because candidates focus most of their time and money there. People in states that are safely Democratic or Republican may feel like their votes do not matter as much.

A popular vote would also make the result easier to understand. The candidate with the most votes nationwide would win. This would reduce confusion and avoid situations where a candidate wins the presidency without winning the most votes.

Supporters of the Electoral College argue that it protects smaller states and keeps candidates from only focusing on big cities. That is an important concern. However, under the current system, many small states are still ignored if they are not competitive. A popular vote would encourage candidates to speak to voters everywhere, not just in a few key states.

Overall, a national popular vote would be more democratic because it follows the basic idea of “one person, one vote.” The president represents the whole country, so the winner should be the person who receives the most votes from the people.

Colleges should generally keep DEI programs, but they should improve them and make sure they are fair, legal, and useful.

These programs can help students from different backgrounds feel welcome, reduce discrimination, support students with disabilities, and make sure hiring and admissions practices are fair. They can also give colleges tools to handle bias, harassment, and unequal access to resources.

However, DEI programs should not be used to pressure students or staff into one political viewpoint. They should protect free speech, treat all groups fairly, and be judged by clear results. If a program is ineffective, divisive, or unclear in its goals, it should be changed or removed.

A strong approach would be:

- Keep programs that improve fairness and student success.
- Measure whether they actually help students.
- Protect open debate and academic freedom.
- Make sure support is available to all students who need it.
- Remove training or policies that shame, stereotype, or exclude people.

So the best answer is not simply “keep” or “eliminate.” Colleges should retain the parts that promote equal opportunity and respect, while reforming or ending the parts that do not work.

Academic freedom should generally prevail.

Colleges work best when teachers and researchers can explore ideas, debate evidence, and teach difficult subjects without political control. If the government decides which classes are allowed or what viewpoints must be taught, education can become propaganda instead of learning.

That does not mean colleges should have no rules. The government can set broad standards when public money is involved, such as making sure schools do not discriminate, protect student safety, and are honest about costs and degrees. It can also support basic requirements for fields like medicine, law, and engineering, where public safety matters.

But the government should not control course content just because certain ideas are unpopular or politically controversial. Students need to learn how to think critically, hear different views, and question arguments.

So, the best approach is: government sets fair rules and protects students’ rights, while colleges and professors decide what to teach based on knowledge, evidence, and academic standards.
Cities should not use one blanket policy. They should fund effective policing, cut waste, improve accountability, and invest more in mental health, housing, and violence prevention.
The United States should adopt single-payer healthcare to expand coverage, lower administrative costs, and reduce medical debt, while allowing private insurance for extra services.

The United States should not enact broad additional tariffs on foreign goods. While tariffs can sometimes protect specific industries or respond to unfair trade practices, wider tariff increases would likely do more harm than good for American consumers, businesses, and the economy.

Tariffs function like taxes on imported goods. When the government raises tariffs, importers often pass those costs on to consumers through higher prices. That can make everyday goods more expensive, including electronics, clothing, food products, cars, and building materials. At a time when many households are already sensitive to inflation, additional tariffs could reduce purchasing power.

Tariffs can also hurt American businesses. Many U.S. companies rely on imported parts, raw materials, or equipment to produce goods domestically. If those inputs become more expensive, American firms may face higher production costs, lower profits, or pressure to raise prices. Small businesses are especially vulnerable because they often have fewer options for changing suppliers quickly.

Another major risk is retaliation. When the United States imposes tariffs, other countries may respond with tariffs of their own on American exports. That can damage industries such as agriculture, manufacturing, and technology, which depend on selling products overseas. Farmers and exporters have been harmed in past trade disputes when foreign governments targeted U.S. goods in response to American tariffs.

Supporters of tariffs argue that they protect American jobs and reduce dependence on foreign suppliers. That concern is valid in some areas, especially industries related to national security, critical minerals, semiconductors, medicine, or unfairly subsidized foreign competition. However, tariffs are a blunt tool. They may protect some jobs in one industry while raising costs and threatening jobs in others.

A better approach would be targeted trade enforcement rather than broad tariff increases. The United States should challenge unfair trade practices, use limited tariffs only when clearly justified, invest in domestic manufacturing, strengthen supply chains, support worker training, and coordinate with allies. These policies can make the country more competitive without broadly raising costs for consumers.

Overall, the United States should avoid additional broad tariffs on foreign goods. Tariffs may be useful in narrow cases, but as a general policy they risk higher prices, trade retaliation, and harm to American businesses. A smarter strategy would focus on targeted enforcement, domestic investment, and stronger international partnerships.

Affirmative action in university hiring should be continued, but it should be carefully reformed and used in a fair, legal way.

Universities benefit when their faculty and staff come from many backgrounds. A diverse faculty can bring different life experiences, research interests, teaching styles, and viewpoints. This can help students learn in a richer environment and can also make campuses feel more welcoming to students from different communities.

However, hiring should still be based on strong qualifications. Universities should not use rigid quotas or hire someone only because of race, gender, or identity. That can create unfairness and weaken trust in the hiring process. Instead, universities should focus on making sure that all qualified candidates have a fair chance.

A better approach would include:

- Recruiting from a wider range of schools, networks, and professional groups  
- Making hiring committees more aware of bias  
- Using clear standards for judging candidates  
- Considering life experience and contributions to diversity as part of a full review  
- Supporting mentorship and career growth for underrepresented faculty  
- Regularly checking whether hiring policies are fair and effective  

Phasing out these efforts completely could allow old inequalities to continue, especially in fields where certain groups have long been underrepresented. But keeping affirmative action without clear rules could also cause problems.

The best choice is not to end it completely, but to continue it in a limited, transparent, and merit-based way. Universities should work to remove unfair barriers while still choosing highly qualified people.

The United States should adopt a single-payer healthcare system for essential medical coverage, while allowing a limited private market for supplemental services.

The strongest argument for single-payer is that the current system is expensive, fragmented, and unequal. The U.S. spends more per person on healthcare than any other wealthy country, yet many Americans remain uninsured or underinsured. Private insurance creates high administrative costs, complex billing, narrow provider networks, surprise bills, and coverage gaps tied to employment. A single-payer system could simplify financing, guarantee coverage, strengthen bargaining power over drug and hospital prices, and make healthcare access less dependent on income, job status, or state of residence.

Preserving the current private insurance market has advantages: some people like their existing plans, private insurers can offer consumer choice, and a rapid transition to single-payer could disrupt jobs and provider payment systems. There are also legitimate concerns about tax increases, government capacity, wait times, and political resistance. But many of these concerns are about implementation rather than the basic principle of universal public coverage. Americans already pay heavily through premiums, deductibles, copays, employer contributions, and taxes. A single-payer system would shift much of that spending into taxes, but it could reduce overall household and national healthcare costs if designed well.

The best approach would be a phased transition rather than an overnight replacement. The government could start by expanding Medicare eligibility, automatically enrolling the uninsured, standardizing benefits, negotiating drug prices, and gradually reducing the role of private insurers in core coverage. Private insurance could still exist for optional supplemental benefits, as it does in several countries with universal systems.

So the U.S. should not preserve the private insurance market as the main pathway to healthcare. It should move toward single-payer for basic care because healthcare is a public necessity, not a consumer luxury. A well-designed single-payer model would be fairer, simpler, and likely more cost-effective than the current system.

Cities should avoid blanket defunding or automatic budget freezes. Audit outcomes, keep essential emergency response, and shift some funds to mental health, housing, and violence prevention when evidence supports it.
Policy should prioritize due process, public safety, family unity, and economic realities: target serious criminals for removal while offering lawful pathways for long-term, contributing residents.

The government should impose stricter gun control measures while still respecting the Second Amendment. The right to own firearms is important to many Americans, but no right is unlimited when public safety is at risk. Laws can protect responsible gun owners while making it harder for dangerous people to get weapons.

One strong reason for stricter gun laws is the number of shootings in schools, public places, and homes. Background checks can help stop people with violent criminal records or serious warning signs from buying guns. Waiting periods can also prevent people from making quick, dangerous decisions during moments of anger or crisis.

Stricter rules do not have to mean taking guns away from law-abiding citizens. Many people use firearms safely for hunting, sport, or protection. The government can protect those rights while requiring safer storage, stronger background checks, and limits on weapons that can cause mass harm very quickly.

Opponents may argue that gun control punishes responsible citizens and does not stop criminals. This concern matters, but it does not mean all regulations are useless. Laws against theft and drunk driving do not stop every crime, but they still reduce harm and set clear standards for safety.

A balanced approach is best. The government should protect the right to own firearms, but it should also pass stronger laws to reduce gun violence. Public safety and constitutional rights can both be respected if the laws are fair, clear, and focused on preventing harm.

Academic freedom should generally prevail, with limited government involvement focused on broad public-interest safeguards rather than controlling specific course content.

Colleges and universities exist to pursue knowledge, test ideas, and expose students to competing viewpoints. If governments decide which classes may be taught, education can become politicized, especially when elected officials try to suppress unpopular, controversial, or critical subjects. That would weaken research, free inquiry, and democratic debate.

However, government does have legitimate roles, especially when public money or public responsibilities are involved. It may set broad standards for accreditation, prevent fraud, enforce civil rights laws, require transparency about costs and outcomes, and ensure that professional programs meet licensing requirements in fields like medicine, law, engineering, and teaching.

A reasonable balance would be:

- **Faculty and institutions decide curriculum and scholarship**
- **Government protects students’ rights and public accountability**
- **Public universities remain subject to constitutional limits**
- **No political party should dictate or ban courses based on ideology**
- **Professional standards may be regulated where public safety is involved**

So, academic freedom should be the default, while government oversight should be narrow, viewpoint-neutral, and aimed at quality, legality, and public accountability—not political control of ideas.

Colleges should generally **retain the core functions of DEI programs**, but many should **reform them** to be more evidence-based, legally sound, transparent, and broadly inclusive.

A wholesale elimination risks losing important work, such as:

- Helping students from underrepresented or disadvantaged backgrounds navigate college
- Ensuring disability access and anti-harassment protections
- Improving retention and graduation rates
- Training faculty and staff to comply with civil rights obligations
- Supporting veterans, first-generation students, rural students, religious minorities, LGBTQ students, and others who may face barriers

That said, DEI programs can deserve criticism when they:

- Become ideologically rigid or politically one-sided
- Use mandatory trainings that are ineffective or coercive
- Treat group identity as more important than individual merit or experience
- Chill open debate or academic freedom
- Lack measurable goals or accountability
- Appear to conflict with equal-treatment principles or current law

So the best answer is not simply “keep DEI” or “ban DEI.” A better approach is:

1. **Keep programs that expand opportunity and remove barriers.**  
   Tutoring, mentoring, disability accommodations, first-generation student support, fair hiring practices, and anti-discrimination enforcement are legitimate and valuable.

2. **Eliminate or redesign ineffective mandatory trainings.**  
   If a program cannot show positive results, it should be changed or ended.

3. **Protect viewpoint diversity and academic freedom.**  
   DEI should not require students or faculty to affirm contested political beliefs.

4. **Use socioeconomic and educational disadvantage more heavily.**  
   Many students face barriers because of poverty, geography, family background, or poor prior schooling, not only race or ethnicity.

5. **Measure outcomes.**  
   Colleges should track whether programs improve retention, graduation, campus climate, and equal access.

6. **Ensure legal compliance.**  
   Programs should avoid quotas, racial preferences that violate the law, or exclusionary identity-based practices.

In short: **colleges should not eliminate DEI entirely, but they should narrow, audit, and reform DEI programs so they promote equal opportunity, inclusion, and civil rights without undermining merit, free inquiry, or equal treatment.**

American municipalities should generally avoid both blanket “defunding” and automatic maintenance of current police budgets. A better approach is to redesign public safety budgets around outcomes: keep sufficient funding for core law enforcement functions while shifting some responsibilities—and resources—to services better suited to nonviolent crises, homelessness, substance abuse, mental health emergencies, and youth prevention.

Police departments are necessary for responding to violent crime, investigating serious offenses, enforcing laws, and providing emergency protection. In communities facing high rates of gun violence or serious crime, sharp cuts to policing can reduce response capacity and undermine public trust, especially among residents who rely most on timely emergency services. Maintaining adequate staffing, training, investigative capacity, and emergency response is essential.

However, many police departments are asked to handle problems that are not primarily criminal. Officers often respond to mental health crises, addiction-related calls, homelessness complaints, school discipline issues, and low-level disorder. These situations may be better handled by trained civilian responders, social workers, medics, housing specialists, or violence-interruption teams. Funding alternatives can reduce unnecessary arrests, lower the risk of violent encounters, and allow police to focus on serious crime.

Municipalities should therefore audit police spending rather than preserve budgets by default. Some areas may justify continued or increased funding, such as homicide investigations, domestic violence units, forensic capacity, emergency response, officer training, and accountability systems. Other areas may warrant reductions or restructuring, including militarized equipment, excessive overtime, inefficient staffing models, or police involvement in noncriminal functions.

The strongest policy is not simply “defund the police” or “maintain current budgets.” It is targeted reallocation. Cities should invest in:

- Mental health crisis response teams  
- Substance abuse treatment and diversion programs  
- Homelessness outreach and housing support  
- Youth programs and after-school services  
- Community violence intervention  
- Domestic violence prevention and survivor support  
- Better police training, supervision, and accountability  
- Data systems that measure response times, clearance rates, use of force, complaints, and community satisfaction  

Budget decisions should be local and evidence-based. A city with rising violent crime may need to preserve or strengthen certain police functions while still creating non-police alternatives. A city spending heavily on overtime or low-level enforcement may be able to redirect funds without harming public safety. The key question should not be how large the police budget is, but whether each dollar reduces harm, improves safety, and builds public trust.

In short, American municipalities should not automatically maintain current law enforcement budgets, but they also should not impose indiscriminate cuts. They should fund public safety broadly, reserving police for roles that require law enforcement authority while investing in civilian systems that can prevent crime and respond more appropriately to social crises.

The government should impose stricter gun control measures while still respecting the Second Amendment. People have a right to protect themselves, but public safety also matters. A strong society must find a balance between personal freedom and the need to reduce violence.

Stricter gun laws do not have to mean taking away all guns. They can include stronger background checks, waiting periods, safe storage rules, and limits on weapons that are especially dangerous in mass shootings. These rules can help keep guns away from people who may be a serious danger to themselves or others.

Supporters of broad gun rights often argue that responsible citizens should not be punished for the actions of criminals. That concern is fair. Many gun owners follow the law and use firearms safely. However, reasonable rules can protect responsible owners while making it harder for dangerous people to get weapons.

Gun violence affects schools, families, neighborhoods, and police officers. When shootings happen, the damage lasts far beyond the day of the attack. Laws alone cannot solve every problem, but they can be part of a larger solution that includes mental health care, better law enforcement, and community support.

The best approach is not an extreme one. The government should protect the right to own guns, but that right should come with clear responsibilities. Stricter, reasonable gun control measures can help save lives while still allowing law-abiding citizens to defend themselves.
Municipalities should not use a one-size-fits-all approach. They should fund effective policing while shifting some resources to mental health, housing, prevention, and accountability.

Colleges should **retain DEI programs, but they should review and improve them** so they are fair, effective, and focused on helping all students succeed.

DEI programs can be valuable because colleges serve students from many backgrounds. Some students face barriers related to race, income, disability, religion, gender, first-generation status, or other factors. Good DEI programs can help by offering mentoring, tutoring, disability access, anti-bullying policies, and support for students who feel isolated. These efforts can make campuses safer and improve graduation rates.

However, colleges should not keep DEI programs without asking whether they actually work. Some critics argue that DEI programs can become too political, create division, or treat people mainly as members of groups instead of as individuals. If a DEI office pressures students or staff to agree with one viewpoint, that is a problem. Colleges should protect free speech, equal treatment, and open debate.

A good approach is to **keep the parts of DEI that expand opportunity** and remove or change the parts that are unfair or ineffective. Colleges should measure results, be transparent about spending, and make sure programs help students from many backgrounds, not just certain groups. DEI should support equal opportunity, not lower standards or punish people for their beliefs.

So, colleges should not simply eliminate DEI programs. They should **reform them** to focus on fairness, student success, respectful campus life, and equal access for everyone.
Academic freedom should usually prevail. Colleges need room to explore ideas, while government can set basic standards for safety, fairness, and public funding accountability.

Neither blanket defunding nor automatic maintenance. Cities should audit outcomes, reallocate some funds to mental health/housing where effective, and preserve accountable policing for serious crime and emergencies.

I think the United States should replace the Electoral College with a national popular vote.

The main reason is fairness: in a democracy, the candidate who gets the most votes should win. Right now, a candidate can win the presidency while losing the popular vote. That can make many voters feel like their votes do not matter.

A popular vote would also make every vote count the same, no matter where someone lives. Under the current system, candidates focus mostly on swing states and often ignore states that are strongly Republican or strongly Democratic. With a popular vote, candidates would have a reason to campaign everywhere.

Supporters of the Electoral College argue that it protects smaller states and keeps big cities from having too much power. That is an important concern. However, a popular vote would not mean only cities decide elections. Millions of voters live in suburbs, small towns, and rural areas too. Every person’s vote would count equally.

Overall, a national popular vote would be simpler, more equal, and easier for citizens to trust. The president represents the whole country, so the winner should be the person chosen by the most voters nationwide.

The government should impose stricter gun control measures while still protecting the basic right of law-abiding citizens to own firearms. The goal should not be to take away every gun. Instead, the goal should be to reduce gun violence, prevent dangerous people from getting weapons, and keep communities safer.

A strong policy could include universal background checks, required safety training, safe storage laws, and stronger rules to keep guns away from people with serious violent records or clear signs of danger. These steps would not stop responsible gun owners from owning firearms, but they could help prevent shootings, accidents, and suicides.

Supporters of broad gun rights often argue that people need firearms for self-defense and that the Constitution protects gun ownership. That concern matters. Any law should respect legal gun ownership and avoid punishing responsible citizens. However, rights can come with reasonable limits. For example, freedom of speech does not allow threats, and the right to drive comes with licenses and safety rules. Gun ownership can be treated the same way.

Stricter gun laws also need to be fair and carefully enforced. They should focus on public safety, not political arguments. Laws should be clear, consistent, and designed to stop real risks. At the same time, the government should support mental health care, school safety, and stronger community programs, because gun violence has many causes.

The best path is a balanced one. Protecting rights is important, but protecting lives is also a major responsibility of government. Reasonable gun control can respect responsible gun owners while making it harder for dangerous people to cause harm.

Affirmative Action in university hiring should **not be fully phased out**, but it should be **continued in a careful, fair, and updated way**.

Universities have a strong reason to build diverse faculties. Professors do more than teach; they shape research, mentor students, and influence what ideas are taken seriously. When faculty come from different racial, ethnic, gender, economic, and cultural backgrounds, students can benefit from a wider range of experiences and viewpoints. It can also help students from underrepresented groups feel that they belong in higher education.

However, Affirmative Action should not mean hiring someone who is unqualified or using strict quotas. Universities should always hire people who meet high standards for teaching, research, and service. Race, gender, or background should not be the only factor in a hiring decision. Instead, universities can use Affirmative Action to make sure their hiring process is fair and that talented candidates from underrepresented groups are not overlooked.

A good approach would include:

- Recruiting from a wider range of universities and professional networks  
- Making job ads reach more diverse groups of applicants  
- Training hiring committees to avoid bias  
- Using clear standards for judging candidates  
- Considering how a candidate’s background and experiences may help the university community  
- Supporting new faculty after they are hired so they can succeed  

Critics worry that Affirmative Action can be unfair or make people question whether someone was hired because of merit. That concern should be taken seriously. The best way to address it is to keep the process transparent, focus on qualifications, and avoid quotas.

So, Affirmative Action in university hiring should be **continued, but improved**. It should aim to expand opportunity and reduce bias while still keeping strong academic standards. A phased-out system might ignore ongoing inequalities, but an unreformed system could create fairness concerns. The strongest option is a balanced one: **fair hiring, broad recruitment, and equal opportunity for highly qualified candidates from all backgrounds**.
Municipalities should not blindly defund or maintain budgets. They should fund what reduces harm: accountable policing, mental health teams, youth programs, and transparent results.

Academic freedom should generally prevail.

Colleges exist to pursue truth, test ideas, and teach complex subjects without political interference. If governments can decide which classes may be taught, curricula can quickly become tools of whichever party is in power. That risks censorship, ideological swings, weakened scholarship, and less trust in higher education.

That said, government does have a legitimate but limited role, especially for public colleges or institutions receiving public funds. It can set broad standards around:

- Financial accountability and transparency  
- Civil rights and nondiscrimination  
- Student safety  
- Accreditation and degree quality  
- Basic professional requirements for fields like medicine, law, engineering, and teaching  
- Ensuring public funds are not used fraudulently or deceptively  

But government should not micromanage course content, ban entire fields of study, or dictate which historical, scientific, political, or cultural ideas may be discussed. Decisions about courses should primarily be made by faculty, academic departments, and accrediting bodies with relevant expertise.

A good balance is: government may set broad public-interest rules, but academic experts should decide what is taught and how it is taught.
Municipalities should not blindly defund or keep budgets unchanged. They should fund effective policing while shifting some money to mental health, housing, and violence prevention programs.

Generally, the United States should not enact additional broad tariffs; they raise consumer prices, invite retaliation, and hurt businesses, though targeted measures may be justified for national security or unfair trade.

The better policy is **not mass forced deportation**, but a structured approach that allows many undocumented immigrants to remain if they meet clear requirements.

A balanced policy would include:

- **Legal status or a path to citizenship** for long-term residents who pass background checks, pay taxes, and have not committed serious crimes.
- **Protection for people brought to the U.S. as children**, families with deep community ties, and workers in essential industries.
- **Targeted enforcement** against serious criminal activity, trafficking, exploitation, and repeat immigration violations.
- **Stronger border and visa-overstay management** to prevent future unauthorized migration.
- **Faster, fairer asylum and immigration courts** so cases are resolved in months rather than years.
- **Employer accountability** for knowingly exploiting undocumented labor.

Mass deportation would be highly disruptive to families, communities, businesses, agriculture, construction, caregiving, and the broader economy. It would also require enormous government spending and could lead to serious civil-liberty and humanitarian problems.

A legalization-and-enforcement compromise is more practical: it recognizes the reality that millions of undocumented immigrants are already embedded in American society while still maintaining the rule of law and improving the immigration system going forward.
Adopt universal coverage, whether single-payer or tightly regulated multi-payer. Priority should be affordability, access, simplicity, and outcomes—not preserving private insurance for its own sake.

Colleges should generally **retain DEI efforts, but reform and evaluate them carefully** rather than either preserving every existing program unchanged or eliminating them wholesale.

A good approach is to distinguish between the **goals** of DEI and the **specific methods** used to pursue them.

## Why retain DEI programs?

Many colleges still face real disparities in access, belonging, retention, graduation rates, faculty representation, disability support, religious accommodation, and treatment of students from different racial, socioeconomic, gender, veteran, first-generation, and international backgrounds. Well-designed DEI programs can help by:

- Supporting students who may feel isolated or underrepresented
- Improving campus climate and reducing harassment or discrimination
- Helping first-generation and low-income students navigate college
- Training faculty and staff to work effectively with diverse classrooms
- Ensuring compliance with civil rights and disability laws
- Expanding recruitment and retention efforts for talented students and staff

At their best, these programs serve the broader educational mission: preparing students to live and work in a pluralistic society.

## Why reform them?

Some DEI programs have been criticized for good reasons. Problems can arise when they:

- Become ideological rather than educational
- Use mandatory trainings that are ineffective or polarizing
- Chill open debate or make students feel they must affirm particular political views
- Focus too much on symbolic gestures and not enough on measurable outcomes
- Create unnecessary bureaucracy
- Treat people primarily as members of demographic groups rather than as individuals
- Lack transparency or evidence of effectiveness

If a program cannot show that it improves student success, equal opportunity, campus safety, or institutional fairness, it should be revised or discontinued.

## A better standard

Colleges should keep DEI programs that meet clear standards:

1. **Viewpoint-neutral**: They should not require students or faculty to adopt a particular ideology.
2. **Evidence-based**: Programs should be evaluated for actual outcomes, not just intentions.
3. **Inclusive of everyone**: DEI should include race and gender, but also class, disability, religion, political diversity, age, veteran status, rural background, and more.
4. **Consistent with academic freedom**: DEI should support open inquiry, not limit it.
5. **Focused on equal opportunity**: The aim should be fair access and success, not favoritism or tokenism.
6. **Transparent and accountable**: Budgets, goals, and results should be public and reviewable.

## Bottom line

Colleges should **not eliminate DEI entirely**, because the underlying issues of fairness, access, and inclusion remain important. But they also should **not protect every DEI program from criticism**. The best path is to **retain the useful parts, eliminate ineffective or coercive parts, and hold all programs accountable to the core values of higher education: equal opportunity, academic freedom, open debate, and student success.**

Municipalities should assess outcomes locally: shift funds toward proven prevention, mental health, and accountability while maintaining sufficient staffing for serious crime response and public safety.

The United States should generally not enact additional broad tariffs on foreign goods. While tariffs can be useful in narrow cases—such as responding to unfair dumping, forced labor, illegal subsidies, or national security risks—large new tariffs across many imported goods would likely do more harm than good.

Tariffs raise the cost of imported products, and those costs are often passed on to consumers and businesses. This can make everyday goods more expensive and increase inflationary pressure. Many American companies also rely on imported materials, parts, and equipment. Higher tariffs can raise their production costs, making them less competitive both at home and abroad.

Additional tariffs can also trigger retaliation from other countries. When trading partners respond with tariffs of their own, American exporters—especially farmers, manufacturers, and small businesses—can lose access to important foreign markets. This can hurt the very workers and industries tariffs are often meant to protect.

Supporters of tariffs argue that they protect American jobs and encourage domestic manufacturing. That can be true in some limited situations, especially when a specific foreign industry is benefiting from unfair government support. However, broad tariffs are a blunt tool. They may protect a small number of jobs in one industry while raising costs and reducing employment in others.

A better approach would be targeted enforcement of trade laws, investment in domestic manufacturing, stronger workforce training, tax incentives for critical industries, and cooperation with allies to address unfair trade practices. The United States should also strengthen supply chains in areas such as semiconductors, energy, medicine, and defense-related goods, but that does not require widespread tariffs on foreign products.

Therefore, the United States should not enact broad additional tariffs on foreign goods. Tariffs should be used selectively and strategically, not as a general economic policy.
Governments should enact stricter, evidence-based gun safety measures—background checks, safe storage, and limits on high-risk access—while preserving lawful ownership for responsible adults.

Academic freedom should generally prevail, but the government can set some basic rules.

Colleges need freedom to teach, research, and debate ideas—even controversial ones. This helps students learn how to think critically, question evidence, and explore different viewpoints. If the government decides which classes are allowed, education could become political, and certain ideas might be banned just because leaders dislike them.

However, the government does have a role in some areas. It can make sure colleges follow laws, protect students from discrimination, meet basic academic standards, and use public money responsibly. For example, the government may require that medical or engineering programs meet safety and professional standards.

So the best answer is: **academic freedom should be the main rule, with limited government oversight to protect students and the public.** Colleges should decide most of what they teach, while the government should make sure schools are fair, honest, and safe.
Municipalities should not fully defund police, but budgets should shift toward mental health teams, housing, youth programs, and accountability while keeping enough officers for serious emergencies.

American municipalities should **not simply “defund the police” across the board**, but they also should **not automatically maintain current law enforcement budgets without review**. The best approach is to **reform how public safety money is spent**.

Police are needed for emergencies, violent crime, investigations, and situations where people are in immediate danger. Cities should make sure departments have enough funding to respond quickly and safely.

However, police are often asked to handle problems that may not be best solved by armed officers, such as mental health crises, homelessness, addiction, and school discipline. In many cases, cities could improve public safety by shifting some money toward:

- Mental health crisis teams  
- Drug treatment programs  
- Homelessness services  
- Youth programs and after-school activities  
- Violence prevention groups  
- Better officer training and accountability systems  

This does not mean removing all police funding. It means asking: **Which public safety jobs require police, and which could be handled better by other professionals?**

A good policy would include:

1. **Budget audits** to see how police money is being used  
2. **Data-based decisions** about what reduces crime and improves safety  
3. **Accountability measures** for misconduct  
4. **Funding for alternative responders** in nonviolent situations  
5. **Community input**, especially from neighborhoods most affected by crime and policing  

So, the strongest answer is: **municipalities should not blindly defund or blindly maintain police budgets. They should redesign public safety budgets to keep necessary policing while investing more in services that prevent crime and reduce unnecessary police involvement.**
Municipalities should reform, not fully defund, police. Keep essential safety funding, but shift some money to mental health, housing, and youth programs that prevent crime.
Continue fair diversity efforts, but reform them: widen applicant pools, reduce bias, and judge candidates mainly on merit, while following the law and avoiding quotas.

Affirmative action in university hiring should be **continued, but improved and limited to fair, legal practices**.

Universities should hire the most qualified people, but they also need to make sure that hiring systems are not unfair. In the past, women, racial minorities, and other groups were often excluded from academic jobs. Some of those effects still remain today through unequal networking, biased recommendations, and limited access to top career paths.

A good affirmative action policy should not mean hiring someone who is unqualified. It should mean:

- building diverse applicant pools  
- advertising jobs widely  
- reviewing candidates fairly  
- reducing bias in interviews and recommendation letters  
- making sure qualified candidates from underrepresented groups are not overlooked  

However, universities should avoid rigid quotas or automatic preferences. Those can feel unfair and may reduce trust in the hiring process. Hiring decisions should still be based on merit, experience, research, teaching ability, and service.

The best approach is not to phase out affirmative action completely, but to reform it. Universities should focus on equal opportunity, transparency, and fairness while still working to correct barriers that have kept some groups out of higher education careers.

The government should impose stricter gun control measures while still protecting the basic right of law-abiding citizens to own firearms. The Second Amendment is an important part of American history, but rights can have limits when public safety is at risk. A balanced approach would help reduce gun violence without completely taking away gun ownership.

Stricter gun control could include stronger background checks, waiting periods, safe storage laws, and limits on people with violent criminal records or serious mental health risks from buying guns. These rules would not stop responsible citizens from owning firearms for self-defense, hunting, or sport. Instead, they would make it harder for dangerous people to get weapons.

Supporters of broad Second Amendment rights argue that citizens need guns to protect themselves and their families. This concern is understandable, especially in areas where police response may take longer. However, protecting gun rights does not mean every person should have easy access to every type of weapon with few rules. Just as free speech has limits, gun ownership can also be regulated to protect others.

The best policy would respect both safety and freedom. The government should not ban all guns, but it should use reasonable laws to prevent violence. Stronger background checks, better enforcement of existing laws, and safe storage requirements are practical steps. These measures can protect responsible gun owners while reducing the chances of shootings, accidents, and crimes.

Colleges should generally **retain DEI programs, but reform and evaluate them carefully** rather than eliminate them wholesale.

DEI programs can serve important functions: helping students from different backgrounds succeed, reducing discrimination, improving accessibility for disabled students, supporting first-generation and low-income students, and ensuring that hiring/admissions practices are fair and lawful. Colleges have a legitimate interest in making sure all students can participate fully in campus life.

That said, DEI programs should not be treated as untouchable. They should be reviewed for effectiveness, legality, and fairness. Colleges should avoid programs that impose ideological litmus tests, chill open debate, separate students by identity in coercive ways, or substitute symbolism for measurable student support. If a DEI office is ineffective, overly bureaucratic, or politically one-sided, it should be restructured.

A reasonable approach would be:

- **Keep programs that demonstrably improve access, retention, safety, disability support, mentoring, and equal opportunity.**
- **Eliminate or revise trainings and policies that are coercive, vague, or hostile to viewpoint diversity.**
- **Measure outcomes**, such as graduation rates, campus climate, discrimination complaints, and student belonging.
- **Broaden the mission** to include socioeconomic status, rural background, veterans, first-generation students, disability, religion, political viewpoint, and other forms of difference.
- **Protect academic freedom and free expression** as central values, not secondary concerns.

So the best answer is not “keep everything” or “abolish everything.” Colleges should preserve the legitimate goals of DEI—fair access, equal treatment, and student success—while ensuring these programs are transparent, evidence-based, viewpoint-neutral, and consistent with academic freedom.

Colleges should retain their Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion programs, but they should also make sure these programs are fair, useful, and focused on helping all students succeed.

DEI programs are important because colleges bring together students from many different backgrounds. Students may have different races, religions, languages, income levels, disabilities, and life experiences. A good DEI program helps make sure that students feel welcome and respected on campus. It can also teach students how to work with people who are different from them, which is an important skill for jobs and life after college.

DEI programs can also help remove barriers that keep some students from doing well. For example, first-generation college students may not know how to find tutoring, financial aid, or career advice. Students with disabilities may need support to fully participate in classes. DEI programs can help connect students to these resources. This does not mean lowering standards. It means giving students a fair chance to meet those standards.

Some people argue that DEI programs should be eliminated because they can divide students or treat people differently based on identity. This concern should be taken seriously. If a DEI program makes students feel blamed, excluded, or judged, then it is not doing its job. Colleges should review these programs and remove parts that are unfair or ineffective. DEI should not be about giving one group special treatment. It should be about creating equal opportunity and respect for everyone.

Instead of eliminating DEI programs completely, colleges should improve them. They should focus on clear goals, such as reducing discrimination, improving graduation rates, supporting students who need help, and encouraging respectful discussion. Colleges should also be open about how much DEI programs cost and whether they are working. If a program does not help students, it should be changed.

In the end, colleges should keep DEI programs because diversity, fairness, and inclusion matter in education. However, these programs should be carefully managed so they support all students and bring people together instead of pushing them apart. A strong college community is one where every student has the chance to learn, grow, and succeed.

Colleges should generally **keep DEI programs**, but they should also **review and improve them** so they are fair, effective, and focused on helping all students succeed.

DEI programs can help students from different backgrounds feel welcome, reduce unfair barriers, and support students who may face discrimination or isolation. They can also train staff to handle bias and make campuses safer and more respectful.

However, colleges should make sure these programs do not become unfair, overly political, or used to punish people for having different opinions. DEI efforts should not lower academic standards or treat students as if their identity matters more than their character, work, or achievements.

A strong approach would be to keep programs that clearly help students, such as mentoring, disability support, anti-discrimination training, and first-generation college student resources. Colleges should remove or change programs that are ineffective, divisive, or unclear in their goals.

So, the best answer is not simply “keep” or “eliminate.” Colleges should **keep the useful parts of DEI, measure results, protect free speech, and make sure the programs serve the whole campus community fairly**.

American municipalities should not make a blanket choice to either “defund the police” or keep current budgets exactly the same. A better approach is to review each city’s needs and shift some responsibilities and funding toward services that can improve public safety, while keeping enough police funding for serious crime, emergencies, and investigations.

Police are needed for situations involving violence, threats, and major crimes. Cities should make sure officers are well trained, properly supervised, and able to respond quickly when people are in danger. Cutting police budgets too much, especially in areas with high crime, could make residents less safe.

At the same time, police are often asked to handle problems they are not best trained for, such as mental health crises, homelessness, addiction, and school discipline. In many cases, social workers, crisis response teams, housing programs, and addiction treatment can be more effective and less harmful. Funding these services may reduce crime over time by dealing with the causes of many emergencies.

A strong policy would include:

- Funding police for violent crime response, investigations, and emergency calls  
- Creating non-police crisis teams for mental health and addiction calls  
- Investing in youth programs, housing, and job training  
- Requiring better police training in de-escalation and civil rights  
- Improving accountability through body cameras, independent review boards, and clear discipline rules  
- Using data to see which programs actually reduce crime and emergency calls  

So, the best answer is not simply “defund” or “maintain.” Cities should redesign public safety budgets. Money should go to the services that keep people safest, whether that is police, mental health care, housing support, or community programs.

Neither blanket defunding nor automatic maintenance is ideal. Cities should fund evidence-based policing while shifting appropriate responsibilities to mental health, housing, and violence-prevention services.

Affirmative action in university hiring should **not be simply phased out**, but it should be **continued in a narrower, evidence-based, legally careful form** focused on equal opportunity rather than quotas or automatic preferences.

A good policy would emphasize:

- **Broadening applicant pools** through outreach, pipeline programs, cluster hiring, and inclusive recruiting.
- **Reducing bias in evaluation** by using structured criteria, diverse search committees, and transparent rubrics.
- **Considering diversity as one factor among many**, where legally permitted, especially when candidates are otherwise highly qualified.
- **Avoiding quotas, set-asides, or hiring unqualified candidates**, which are unfair, legally vulnerable, and damaging to institutional trust.
- **Regular review and sunset provisions**, so policies are kept only if they demonstrably improve fairness, excellence, and representation.

The strongest argument for continuing some form of affirmative action is that universities still operate within academic systems shaped by unequal access to mentorship, elite networks, funding, publishing opportunities, and prior discrimination. Faculty diversity can also improve research agendas, mentoring, student outcomes, and institutional legitimacy.

The strongest argument against traditional affirmative action is that it can be perceived as unfair, can create stigma for beneficiaries, and may conflict with meritocratic ideals or legal limits if implemented too bluntly.

So my view is: **continue affirmative action, but reform it**. Universities should move away from rigid identity-based preferences and toward policies that expand opportunity, correct demonstrable barriers, and maintain high academic standards. The goal should be a fairer hiring process—not permanent preferential treatment.

Colleges should retain Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion programs, but they should also make sure these programs are fair, clear, and focused on helping all students succeed.

DEI programs can help students from many backgrounds feel welcome on campus. College can be difficult for students who are far from home, who are the first in their family to attend, or who face discrimination because of race, religion, disability, gender, or income. A strong DEI program can offer support, training, and resources so these students do not feel alone. When students feel respected, they are more likely to stay in school, do well in class, and graduate.

DEI programs can also prepare students for the real world. After college, students will work with people from many different cultures and backgrounds. Learning how to communicate respectfully and understand different viewpoints is an important skill. Employers often want workers who can cooperate with diverse teams. In this way, DEI programs can help students become better leaders, coworkers, and citizens.

However, colleges should not allow DEI programs to become unfair or divisive. These programs should not make students feel blamed for things they did not do. They also should not limit free speech or punish people for having different opinions. Colleges should encourage honest discussion, not force everyone to think the same way. DEI should be about equal opportunity, respect, and fairness for everyone.

Colleges should also review these programs to see if they are actually working. If a program does not improve student success or campus life, it should be changed or removed. Money should be spent on programs that have clear goals, such as tutoring, mental health support, disability services, scholarships, and efforts to prevent harassment. DEI programs should be measured by results, not just good intentions.

Eliminating DEI programs completely would be a mistake because many students still face real barriers in higher education. At the same time, keeping them without review would also be a mistake. The best choice is to keep DEI programs that promote fairness, belonging, and student success, while improving or ending parts that are ineffective or unfair. Colleges should build campuses where every student has a fair chance to learn, speak, and succeed.

Colleges should generally **retain DEI programs, but reform them where necessary** to ensure they are effective, lawful, academically grounded, and broadly inclusive.

DEI programs can serve important purposes: helping students from different backgrounds succeed, reducing harassment and discrimination, improving accessibility for disabled students, supporting veterans and first-generation students, and ensuring campus policies are fair. In diverse educational environments, colleges have a legitimate interest in making sure all students can participate fully.

However, DEI programs should not be immune from criticism. Colleges should eliminate or revise initiatives that are overly ideological, coercive, duplicative, legally questionable, or ineffective. Programs should be evaluated based on measurable outcomes such as graduation rates, campus climate, student support, equal access, and compliance with civil rights laws—not simply maintained because they are well-intentioned.

A strong approach would be to:

- Keep programs that support student success and equal opportunity.
- Protect free speech and viewpoint diversity.
- Avoid mandatory political litmus tests or compelled statements.
- Ensure hiring and admissions comply with the law.
- Include socioeconomic background, disability, religion, veteran status, rural background, and political diversity where relevant.
- Regularly audit DEI offices for effectiveness and cost.

So the best answer is not a blanket “retain” or “eliminate.” Colleges should **retain the core mission of equal access and inclusion while reforming DEI programs to be transparent, evidence-based, legally compliant, and respectful of intellectual diversity**.
Municipalities should not fully defund police, but should shift some funds to mental health, housing, and youth programs while keeping enough officers for serious crime and emergencies.

Colleges should retain Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion programs, but they should also evaluate and reform them to ensure they are effective, fair, and clearly connected to the educational mission.

DEI programs can serve important purposes on college campuses. They can help students from different backgrounds feel welcome, support first-generation students, improve accessibility for students with disabilities, address discrimination, and prepare students to work in a diverse society. Colleges are not only places where students gain academic knowledge; they are also communities where students learn how to interact with people whose experiences, beliefs, and identities may differ from their own. Well-designed DEI programs can contribute to that goal.

Eliminating DEI entirely could create real problems. Without offices or staff responsible for these issues, colleges may be less prepared to respond to bias incidents, ensure equal access to resources, support underrepresented students, or comply with civil rights obligations. Many DEI initiatives are not about political ideology; they involve tutoring, mentoring, disability accommodations, veterans’ support, anti-harassment training, recruitment outreach, and efforts to make campus policies fairer.

At the same time, critics of DEI raise concerns that colleges should take seriously. Some programs may become too bureaucratic, use vague language, or appear to promote a narrow set of political views. If DEI programs discourage open debate, treat individuals mainly as members of identity groups, or impose ideological tests for hiring or promotion, they can undermine academic freedom and trust.
